title: Our Course Project
text:
Spatial Risk Assessment and Urban Transformation Prioritization for Istanbul: A Data-Driven Approach




Yusuf Bora Çavdar
Onur Furkan Gök




The primary objective of this project is to develop a Decision Support Model to identify and prioritize neighborhoods in Istanbul for urban transformation. By integrating building age, official structural needs, and population density, the project aims to calculate a “Seismic Vulnerability Index.” This model will help in optimizing the allocation of urban renewal resources to the areas with the highest potential impact on human life and safety.



The analysis will be grounded on three primary datasets sourced from the IBB Open Data Portal and TUIK:

Building Inventory Dataset: Contains neighborhood-level data on the number of buildings categorized by construction year (pre-1980, 1980-2000, post-2000) and floor counts.
Urban Transformation Requirement Data: Provides official records of buildings identified as “In Need of Transformation” vs. “Not in Need” across districts.
Municipal Population Dataset (2019-2024): A time-series dataset showing population distribution and density across Istanbul’s 39 districts.




The project will follow a structured Data Science workflow:

Data Preprocessing: Cleaning and merging the datasets using dplyr and tidyr. Handling “Wide” to “Long” format conversions for population trends.
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Visualizing the distribution of aged buildings and population hotspots using ggplot2.
Index Calculation: Implementing a weighted risk formula to score each neighborhood/district:
RiskIndex​=(Age_Factor×0.4)+(Transformation_Need×0.3)+(Population_Density×0.3)
Final Output: A dynamic Quarto report hosted on GitHub Pages, featuring an interactive risk ranking and priority map of Istanbul.

title: Yusuf Bora Çavdar's Analytics Lab
text:
About Me
Industrial Engineering Student • Data Analytics Enthusiast • Research-Oriented Thinker

I am passionate about using data-driven insights to optimize processes, support decision-making, and solve complex engineering problems.



Professional Bio
I am Yusuf Bora Çavdar, a third-year Industrial Engineering student at Hacettepe University, expected to graduate in 2026.
My primary academic interests include:


Data Science


Data Analytics


Process Optimization


Statistical Analysis


Decision Support Systems


I am particularly interested in the applications of analytics within Human Resources, especially in Training and Development.
Additionally, I have a strong interest in Lean and Six Sigma methodologies.


Areas of Interest
Data Analytics Machine Learning Lean Six Sigma Statistical Modeling Human Resources Analytics Process Improvement


Activities & Personal Development


Financial Literacy Society

Active member interested in financial management and economic analysis.



Scouting

Focused on leadership, teamwork, discipline, and outdoor skills through scouting activities.



Language Learning

Currently learning German to improve international communication and professional opportunities.



Curriculum Vitae
You can find my detailed academic background, experiences, and achievements in my CV.

I am passionate about using data-driven insights to optimize processes and solve complex engineering problems.
I am particularly interested in exploring the intersections of data analytics within Human Resources, focusing on Training and Development. Additionally, I have a strong interest in Lean and Six Sigma methodologies.

title: Yusuf Bora Çavdar's Analytics Lab
text:
Assignment 1 – Task 3
Video Summary & Data Analysis
Industrial Engineering and Data Science integration based on Cem Vardar’s interview.


Summary
I watched the interview with Cem Vardar, who holds a PhD from Arizona State University and has worked at companies such as Intel and Carvana.
The interview mainly focuses on the relationship between Industrial Engineering and Data Science in real-world applications.
According to Vardar, Industrial Engineering is fundamentally about solving problems within complex systems, while Data Science should be viewed as a practical tool for business problem-solving rather than a standalone profession.
One of the most important concepts mentioned in the interview is “Evolutionary Design,” which emphasizes improving systems gradually through small, data-driven changes instead of replacing entire systems at once.
He also highlights that foundational tools such as SQL and Excel remain highly valuable despite rapid technological changes.


Questions


Open-Ended Question

What is Cem Vardar’s “Evolutionary Design” approach, and why does he suggest it instead of replacing an entire system?

Answer
It refers to improving systems incrementally through small and manageable changes. This approach is preferred because complete system replacements are often expensive, risky, and difficult to implement successfully in real business environments.



Multiple-Choice Question

Which tool is described as a “must-have” skill for over 20 years?

Neural Networks
Deep Fakes
SQL (Structured Query Language)
ChatGPT 

Answer

SQL




AI Disclosure
I used Gemini 3 Flash to:


Summarize the interview transcript


Format the questions


Improve clarity and readability


I also used Gemini AI to debug and correct errors in my R code.


Video Link 
Watch the Interview on YouTube



Data Analysis Section
In this section, the polls_us_election_2016 dataset from the dslabs package is analyzed using R.
library(dslabs) library(dplyr)
data(“polls_us_election_2016”) data <- polls_us_election_2016
my_first <- “Bora” my_birth_year <- 2004
k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8 k
if (k %% 2 == 0) { head(data, k) } else { tail(data, k) }
sum(is.na(data))
na_per_column <- sort(colSums(is.na(data)), decreasing = TRUE) head(na_per_column, 8)
new_data <- data %>% mutate( across(where(is.numeric), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), my_birth_year + k, .)), across(where(is.character), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), paste0(my_first, “”, k), .)), across(where(is.factor), ~ { x <- as.character(.) x[is.na(x)] <- paste0(my_first, ””, k) x }) )
if (k %% 2 == 0) { head(new_data, k) } else { tail(new_data, k) }
sum(is.na(new_data)) anyNA(new_data)


Built with R and Quarto

```

title: Assignment 1

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

# Load dataset

data <- polls_us_election_2016

my_birth_year <- 2004

# Replace NA values
new_data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), my_birth_year + k, .)),
    across(where(is.character), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), paste0(my_first, "_", k), .)),
    across(where(is.factor), ~ {
      x <- as.character(.)
      x[is.na(x)] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
      x
    })
  )

title: Yusuf Bora Çavdar's Analytics Lab
text:
My Assignments
Assignments and analytical works developed for the EMU430 Data Analytics course.
This section contains coursework, data analysis projects, visualizations, and reports created throughout the semester.


Course Information
Course: EMU430 – Data Analytics Term: Spring 2026
This page showcases assignments, analytical approaches, and project outcomes developed during the course.
Please use the navigation menu to explore individual assignments and related works.


Topics Covered
Data Cleaning Visualization Statistical Analysis Machine Learning Exploratory Data Analysis Reporting


About These Assignments
The assignments focus on applying analytical thinking and data-driven methodologies to real-world datasets.
Each project includes:


Data preprocessing and preparation


Exploratory analysis


Statistical interpretation


Visualization techniques


Analytical reporting


The goal is to improve both technical and presentation skills in data analytics.


Latest Update
The most recent update to this page was made on 04/04/2026.
More assignments and analytical reports will be added throughout the semester.


Built with R and Quarto

```
